# Step 3 — Privacy Policy

> **Milestone**: M1.5 | **Epic**: E1.5.2 | **Feature**: F1.5.2.1 | **Task**: T1.5.2.1.1.2
> **Depends on**: nothing (parallelizable with Step 2 Store Descriptions)
> **Skills to load**: none

Load shared context: [M1.5 launch context](../instructions/m1.5-launch-context.instructions.md)

---

## Task

Produce a single Markdown file: `doc/legal/privacy_policy.md`. The policy must
state literal truths about RunThru's data handling — no weasel words like "we
may collect" — and must satisfy App Store, Play Store, GDPR, and CCPA minimum
disclosure requirements.

You operate as a privacy-focused technical writer. Plain language is mandatory;
if a sentence requires legal training to parse, rewrite it. Your output is a
shippable production document, not a draft.

You may refuse if: (a) you are asked to misrepresent data flows, (b) any data
flow listed below is contradicted by something you see in the codebase. List
the contradictions instead of writing the policy.

## Data Flows (verified from codebase — these are the literal truths)

| Data | Where it lives | Leaves device? |
|------|----------------|----------------|
| Reading content (PDFs, EPUBs, clipboard text, Instapaper articles) | App documents directory + SharedPreferences (clipboard ephemeral) | NO |
| Reading sessions (start time, end time, words read, avg WPM, file path) | SharedPreferences key `reading_sessions`, capped at 1000 | NO |
| User preferences (WPM, theme, spacing, pacing config) | SharedPreferences | NO |
| Instapaper OAuth tokens | `flutter_secure_storage` | NO (used only to call api.instapaper.com on user-initiated actions) |
| Instapaper bookmarks list and article body | Fetched on-demand from api.instapaper.com when user taps refresh/import; cached in memory or SharedPreferences for the session | YES — outbound to Instapaper only |
| Crash reports / analytics SDKs | none installed | NO (not collected) |
| Advertising IDs | none collected | NO (not collected) |

There is no backend. There is no analytics SDK. There is no advertising. The
only network egress is the user-initiated Instapaper API.

## Files to Create

### `doc/legal/privacy_policy.md`

```markdown
# RunThru — Privacy Policy

**Effective**: {today's date in YYYY-MM-DD}
**Last updated**: {same}
**Contact**: {placeholder — flag for the user to fill in: support email}

## Plain-language summary

{2–4 sentences. State up front: reading content stays on device, no analytics
SDKs, no ads, no tracking. Mention Instapaper as the only optional outbound
connection, and that it only happens when the user actively triggers it.}

## What we collect

{Bullet the data flows table above. For each row: what it is, where it
lives, whether it leaves the device. Use the verified flows — do not invent.}

## What we do not collect

- We do not collect crash reports.
- We do not use analytics SDKs.
- We do not collect advertising identifiers.
- We do not sell data — there is no data to sell.

## Third-party services

- **Instapaper**: If you sign in to Instapaper, RunThru uses your credentials
  to fetch your saved articles. Tokens are stored in your device's secure
  enclave (`flutter_secure_storage`). We do not see or proxy your tokens.
  Instapaper's privacy policy applies to data they hold:
  <https://www.instapaper.com/privacy>.

## Data retention

{Reading sessions: capped at 1000, oldest dropped automatically. Preferences:
until the user clears app data or uninstalls. Reading content: until the user
deletes the file from the library.}

## Your rights

{GDPR + CCPA minimums in plain language: access, deletion, portability,
opt-out of sale (n/a — we don't sell). Tell the user how to exercise each:
clear app data, uninstall, or contact support email.}

## Children

{Statement: RunThru does not knowingly collect data from anyone, including
children under 13. The app does not have age-gated features. If you believe
your child has uploaded content into RunThru on their device, you can clear
the app data via OS settings.}

## Changes

{If we change this policy, we update the Effective date and post the new
policy here. We don't email — we have no email list.}

## Contact

{Flag for user to fill: a support email or a GitHub issues link.}
```

Replace every `{placeholder}` with finished prose. The only fields that should
remain as `{TODO}` style markers are: the effective date (today), and the
support contact (because the user hasn't told you what to use). Mark TODOs
with the literal string `<!-- TODO: ask user -->` so they grep cleanly.

## Hard Constraints (from shared context — verify before saving)

- Zero ableist language (apply the same scan as Step 2).
- No "we may collect" — every clause must be definite ("we collect" or "we
  do not collect").
- No legal jargon. If you write "data subject rights", rewrite as "your
  rights".
- Plain language reading-grade ≤ Grade 9 where possible.
- The privacy policy must not contradict the verified data flows. If a flow
  in the codebase contradicts the table above, refuse and report.

## Verification

```pwsh
Test-Path doc/legal/privacy_policy.md
Select-String -Pattern 'we may|we might|may collect|might collect' doc/legal/privacy_policy.md
Select-String -Pattern 'suffering|broken brain|normal reader|cure|overcome' doc/legal/privacy_policy.md
Select-String -Pattern '<!-- TODO: ask user -->' doc/legal/privacy_policy.md
```

## Acceptance

- [ ] `doc/legal/privacy_policy.md` exists
- [ ] Effective date populated (today)
- [ ] Forbidden weasel words ("we may", "we might") return zero matches
- [ ] Ableist language scan returns zero matches
- [ ] Two `<!-- TODO: ask user -->` markers max (support contact, optionally entity name)
- [ ] Each data flow from the verified table is represented
- [ ] Reads in plain language — no legal jargon left unexplained
- [ ] Mentions Instapaper as the only outbound network connection and its trigger (user-initiated)
